schools.summary: Keil reads the chapter as a summary of David's whole reign; Ellicott and Wesley take Metheg-ammah of Gath, 'the bridle of the metropolis,' now subdued.
Gill: David's rest was brief, and he had other work than temple-building; the war reopens at once.
Clarke records the Vulgate's 'bondage of tribute' and admits no such place as Metheg-ammah is known; Wesley reads it of Gath on Mount Ammah.
Henry: David completes what Samson began and turns the Philistines' own curb against them — a type of the Son of David treading his enemies underfoot.

sides.position: Gath, the chief Philistine city — 'the bridle of the metropolis' (from 1 Chronicles 18:1)
'the bondage of the tribute' (Vulgate) — an unidentifiable phrase for the reversal of Philistine dominion

themes: type of the Son of David

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the measuring-line literally: the prostrate Moabites were measured by a threefold cord, two parts slain, one spared; a partial fulfilment of Numbers 24:17.
Gill: the Moabites, though once David's hosts, were sworn enemies of Israel; the conquest fulfils Balaam's prophecy.
Wesley: Moab were perpetual enemies barred from the congregation; Clarke reads the line as cast over the cities, two-thirds razed, sparing the king's reputation.

debates.question: Does the measuring-line mean two-thirds of the men were slain, or two-thirds of the cities razed?

sides.position: two-thirds of the Moabite men were put to death, one-third spared
the line was cast over the cities — the strong places razed, not the people slaughtered

schools.summary: Zobah's site is undeterminable (not Aleppo or Nisibis, but near Damascus); 'Hadadezer' is the true form; the border being recovered is Hadadezer's own power (1 Chronicles 18:3; 2 Samuel 10:16).
Gill surveys the guesses for Zobah and notes the rabbis debated whether it belonged to the land of Israel.
Wesley refers 'recover his border' to David, claiming the covenant grant of land to the Euphrates (Genesis 15:18).

debates.question: Whose border/'hand' was being recovered at the river?

sides.position: Hadadezer's — he marched to re-establish his own dominion
David's — he went to claim Israel's covenant boundary to the Euphrates

schools.summary: 'Chariots' has dropped from the text; 700 horsemen should be 7,000 (per 1 Chronicles 18:4); David hamstrung the horses, reserving enough for a hundred chariots — an entering wedge toward Deuteronomy 17:16.
Gill reconciles 700 and 7,000 as seven hundred companies of ten horsemen each under a captain.
Wesley reads 'seven hundred companies'; Clarke denies any houghing, reading that David 'disjointed all the chariots' as sound policy and piety.

debates.question: Did David hamstring the horses, or destroy the chariots?

sides.position: he houghed (hamstrung) the horses, keeping enough for a hundred chariots
the verb means he 'disjointed/destroyed the chariots,' not that he maimed horses

schools.summary: The Syrians 'of Damascus' are named from their capital, the most powerful Syrian branch; Ellicott and Gill cite Nicolaus of Damascus (via Josephus) as pagan corroboration of David's victory.
Wesley: 'of Damascus' means those subject to Damascus, the chief city of Syria.
Gill weighs whether the 'Adad' of the pagan historian was Hadadezer himself, first king of Damascus, or a separate ally.
Ellicott and Keil weigh the word 'garrisons' — 'something placed over,' so either officer or military post; Keil settles on military garrisons, consolidating the conquest of Damascus.
Gill places the garrisons in Coele-Syria and notes the Targum's reading of 'governors' set over the cities; the closing refrain gives all glory to God's preservation.

debates.question: Do the 'garrisons' mean soldiers or governors?

sides.position: military posts / garrisons of soldiers
appointed commanders or governors (Targum)

schools.summary: Ellicott keeps 'shields of gold,' paralleling Solomon's (1 Kings 10:17) and their loss to Shishak (1 Kings 14:25), and flags the LXX's mistaken gloss about Shishak.
Gill surveys the versions — golden chains (Aquila), bracelets (LXX), quivers (Syriac), or shields — as the spoil brought to Jerusalem.
Clarke calls them uncertain 'costly ornaments' of the bodyguard; Wesley says they were kept in the armoury, not carried into the field.

debates.question: What were the golden objects, and how were they borne?

sides.position: shields of gold, worn or carried by Hadadezer's picked bodyguard
uncertain ornaments (chains, bracelets, quivers) kept in the royal armoury, not on the field

schools.summary: Betah and Berothai = Tibhath and Chun (1 Chronicles 18:8), unlocatable; the 'exceeding much brass' became Solomon's brazen sea, pillars, and temple vessels; Ellicott notes it was likely bronze or copper.
Wesley allows the two records name the same cities differently, or else four cities in all.
Gill links Berothai with Ezekiel's Berothah and Ptolemy's Barathena.

Hamath was a northern Syrian kingdom on the Orontes, long at war with Hadadezer; Ellicott traces its history from Solomon's tribute to Assyrian capture; Keil reads the embassy as a suit for peace and congratulation.
Henry: Toi secures himself by alliance, and David gains by friendship what he had won by war — 'better get by composition than by compulsion.'
Gill identifies Hamath and its king Toi/Tou, whom the Targum calls king of Antioch.

The ambassador is Joram (Hadoram in 1 Chronicles 18:10); Ellicott calls Joram the Jewish form, an especially honourable embassy by the king's son bringing gifts near to tribute; 'to bless' means to congratulate.
Gill notes the Syriac and Arabic read Joram in Chronicles too.

debates.question: Is the ambassador's name Joram or Hadoram?

sides.position: Joram is the Hebrew (Jewish) form of the same name
Hadoram is original; Joram is a scribal error, being an Israelite name unlikely for an Aramaean

schools.summary: Ellicott: Toi's free gift is singled out because, unlike the spoils, it was no plunder; David, barred from building, makes every provision for the temple.
Gill: David devoted the treasure to sacred uses, chiefly the building of the house of the Lord.

Henry: dedication makes David's victories outshine Alexander's and Caesar's; the temple built of Gentile spoils foreshadows Revelation 21:24, and every conquered lust must likewise be dedicated to God.

'Syria' here vs 'Edom' in 1 Chronicles 18:11 (d/r confusion) — Ellicott holds both were conquered and dedicated; Amalek is the only post-accession mention; Keil argues 'and smote Edom' dropped from v13.
Gill: 'Syria' means Syria of Damascus, distinct from Zobah, and is omitted in the Chronicler's list.

sides.position: both were conquered and dedicated; each record names one where the other omits it
a scribal slip between the similar words Aram and Edom has disturbed the text

schools.summary: 'Syrians' here vs 'Edom' in 1 Chronicles 18:12 and the title of Psalm 60; Ellicott reconstructs one campaign against a confederacy named for Syria yet chiefly Edomite in the valley of salt (the Arabah); Clarke places the valley near Palmyra.
Gill: David 'made himself a name' by his conquests, not (as the rabbis say) by burying the dead.
Henry: the fame fulfils Isaac's blessing on Jacob over Esau (Genesis 27:40), yet David transfers the glory to God — 'it is through God that we do valiantly.'

debates.question: Whom did David smite in the valley of salt, and where was it?

sides.position: a confederacy called 'Syrian' but chiefly Edomite, in the Arabah south of the Dead Sea
the Syrians, in a valley near Palmyra/Tadmor in the wilderness

schools.summary: Keil and Clarke: David garrisoned all Edom, repairing its strong cities and reducing the whole nation to servitude; the survey of conquest is complete.
Gill notes the Targum/Jarchi gloss of governors or tribute-officers set over Edom.
Wesley: all David's victories typify the gospel's triumph over Satan's kingdom — the Son of David riding forth 'conquering and to conquer' (Revelation 6:2).
Henry: David neglected no part of home government, executing judgment and justice to all with industry, access, and impartiality — a type of Christ, faithful and true (Revelation 19:11; Psalm 72:1).
Gill: David reigned over all Israel, the whole promised land now reaching to the Euphrates (Genesis 15:18).
Joab is commander-in-chief; the 'recorder' (mazkir) is a weighty office — Clarke a chronicler, Ellicott a chancellor and royal adviser (cf. 2 Kings 18:18; 1 Kings 4:3), Wesley a treasurer.
Gill: Joab's command rested on kinship and on David's promise that the first to smite the Jebusites should be chief.
Wesley reads the recorder as a treasurer who examined and kept the accounts.

debates.question: What was the office of the 'recorder' (mazkir)?

sides.position: a chronicler/annalist keeping a journal of the king's affairs
a chancellor and royal adviser who registered the king's decrees
a treasurer who examined and kept the accounts

schools.summary: Zadok (of Eleazar) served at Gibeon, Ahimelech before the ark; the crux 'Ahimelech son of Abiathar' contradicts 1 Samuel 22:20 — Keil calls it an unsolved difficulty, Ellicott explains it as father and son sharing both names (cf. Mark 2:26).
Gill: these were not two high priests but the chief priests serving under Abiathar, as Eli's house declined.
Clarke and Wesley: Seraiah was the king's private secretary or secretary of state.

debates.question: How to resolve 'Ahimelech the son of Abiathar' against 1 Samuel 22:20?

sides.position: the difficulty cannot be removed by transposing the names; it stands unsolved
both names were borne by father and son alike, so each is called by either

schools.summary: Benaiah's Cherethites and Pelethites were David's trusted bodyguard; Ellicott allows a tribal (foreign) sense (1 Samuel 30:14) but the Chaldee/Syriac and Keil read appellatives — 'executioners and runners.'
Gill leans to native Israelites who followed David at Ziklag, prefers the Targum's 'archers and slingers,' and rejects the rabbinic reading of Sanhedrin or Urim and Thummim.
Clarke prefers the Targum's 'archers and slingers'; Wesley allows either an office or a country as the source of the names.

debates.question: Who were the Cherethites and Pelethites?

sides.position: a tribal/national name — foreign (Philistine) mercenaries who had embraced Israel's faith
native guards named by weapon — 'archers and slingers' (Targum)
appellatives of office — 'executioners and runners'
